summary: Gruber follows up on the debate over Mastodon server admins preemptively blocking Meta's upcoming Threads platform from federating via ActivityPub. He curates reactions from several commentators who argue that blocking Threads undermines the very openness that decentralized protocols are supposed to embody. Gruber draws parallels to podcasting and email, where big corporate players ultimately made open platforms more popular rather than destroying them. He acknowledges Meta deserves skepticism but argues preemptive defederation is both hypocritical and strategically foolish. He defends the NDA-covered meetings between Meta and Mastodon admins as standard practice for unannounced products. His core question remains whether the Fediverse is meant to be anti-corporate or pro-openness.
key_points: Mastodon admins preemptively blocking Threads contradicts the foundational principle of open, interoperable protocols
Podcasting's 20-year survival as an open medium despite Apple, Amazon, YouTube, and Spotify proves big players can strengthen rather than destroy open platforms
Many long-time Mastodon users secretly prefer its niche status and resist mainstream adoption while claiming to support openness
Meta's ability to bootstrap Threads off Instagram's social graph gives it an enormous onboarding advantage that Mastodon's confusing setup cannot match
Starting Meta on a short leash — federate but defederate if they misbehave — is more rational than preemptive blocking
money_quotes: I think there are a lot of long-time Mastodon users who like the fact that it isn't gaining mainstream traction, and want to keep it that way. But then don't call it 'open'.

The Mastodon server admins who are preemptively promising to defederate Threads are effectively guaranteeing that users on their instances will be isolated from a potential majority of users on the overall platform.

Ultimately the involvement of these big companies has made open podcasting far more popular and enduring than I ever would have imagined 20 years ago, because they brought podcasting to the mass market.
Facebook's Threads app could be something akin to Apple Podcasts for Mastodon.
The idea that NDAs are inherently nefarious is just conspiratorial tinfoil-hat nonsense.
Is the goal of the Fediverse to be anti-corporate/anti-commercial, or to be pro-openness? I think openness is the answer.
Put them on a short leash. They start fucking around, Mastodon instances should start de-federating from their product. I just don't see why they should be blocked preemptively.
key_insight: Preemptively blocking Meta from the Fediverse betrays the open-protocol principles it claims to defend, and history shows that big corporate players on open platforms tend to grow the ecosystem rather than destroy it.
